Isolated strong to severe storms moved through the region. One tornado and isolated wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very weak tornado developed west of the intersection of Decker Road and Dusty Road north of Gray Summit snapping several trees and crossing an open corn field before lifting. It was only on the ground for a total of one minute and less than two tenths of a mile, but the debris from the harvested corn field was lofted nearly 4300 feet in the air and was detected by radar for 15 minutes after the tornado dissipated. The tornado was rated EF0. Path length was 0.18 miles with a max path width of 25 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of freezing drizzle occurred over southern Missouri on February 10-11, 2018. Ice accretions of 0.10 to 0.20 were common across must of southwest Missouri. The precipitation initially began as patchy freezing drizzle during the early morning hours of February 10 and then became more widespread by midday, continuing into Sunday morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous accidents due to ice covered roads with minor injuries.

Snow developed along an Arctic cold front as it sliced southeast across southeast Miissouri during the morning hours. As the front slowed down over the Tennessee Valley late in the day, a weak surface low developed on the front. This caused snow to persist along and south of U.S. Highway 60 through the evening hours. The result was an area of heavy snowfall from Doniphan east to Sikeston and Charleston southward, where 4 to 8 inches was reported. A few heavy snowfall reports included 8.5 inches at East Prairie (Mississippi County), 7 inches at Charleston, and 5 inches at Poplar Bluff. Across the remainder of southeast Missouri, including Cape Girardeau and Perryville, amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Visibility was frequently reduced below one mile in the heavy snowfall area. Much of the snow melted on treated road surfaces during the day, but conditions rapidly deteriorated after dark as temperatures dropped through the 20's. Actual air temperatures fell into the teens as the snow ended. These cold temperatures reduced the effectiveness of road salt, which slowed recovery efforts in the wake of the storm. Scattered traffic accidents were reported, including the Cape Girardeau area. The new snowfall on top of snow from the January 12th storm raised total snow depths to near a foot in the heavy snowfall area.
Isolated severe thunderstorms produced a few reports of wind damage and a brief EF-1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS storm survey found that a brief EF-1 tornado touched down in rural Lawrence County south of Mount Vernon. A couple of farm buildings were blown down on Farm Road 2155. One barn was destroyed. At least one home had minor roof damage. Several trees were uprooted and blown down along the path. A large tree smashed two cars. Estimated maximum wind speed was up to 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure over the central Plains brought bitterly cold temperatures and gusty west winds to start the new year. Wind chill indices from minus 10 to minus 17 were observed across southeast Missouri during the early and mid-morning hours of the New Year's Day holiday. Actual air temperatures fell into the single digits on New Year's morning, including a low of 2 above zero at the Cape Girardeau airport and 4 above at Poplar Bluff. Winds gusted over 20 mph at times. Some water main breaks occurred during the arctic outbreak. Some warming centers were open for homeless persons to shelter from the cold.

Snow developed along an Arctic cold front as it sliced southeast across southeast Miissouri during the morning hours. As the front slowed down over the Tennessee Valley late in the day, a weak surface low developed on the front. This caused snow to persist along and south of U.S. Highway 60 through the evening hours. The result was an area of heavy snowfall from Doniphan east to Sikeston and Charleston southward, where 4 to 8 inches was reported. A few heavy snowfall reports included 8.5 inches at East Prairie (Mississippi County), 7 inches at Charleston, and 5 inches at Poplar Bluff. Across the remainder of southeast Missouri, including Cape Girardeau and Perryville, amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Visibility was frequently reduced below one mile in the heavy snowfall area. Much of the snow melted on treated road surfaces during the day, but conditions rapidly deteriorated after dark as temperatures dropped through the 20's. Actual air temperatures fell into the teens as the snow ended. These cold temperatures reduced the effectiveness of road salt, which slowed recovery efforts in the wake of the storm. Scattered traffic accidents were reported, including the Cape Girardeau area. The new snowfall on top of snow from the January 12th storm raised total snow depths to near a foot in the heavy snowfall area.

Heavy rainfall over several days caused minor flooding across the Missouri Ozarks. Between four and eight inches of rainfall fell over the course of about a week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused Cowskin Creek to flood a section of Highway Y approximately three miles west northwest of Ava.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 1, 2018 temperatures across Kansas City dropped to -11. Light northwest winds contributed to wind chill values across the city dropping to 25 degrees below zero. As a result of the extreme cold at least one homeless person died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kansas City Health Department confirmed the fatality of a homeless person, with the extremely cold wind chill values being a primary cause of the fatality.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of January 12, 2018 temperatures across Kansas City dropped to the single digits, but light northwest winds contributed to wind chill values across the city dropping to 11 degrees below zero. As a result of the cold at least one homeless person died.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kansas City Health Department confirmed that a homeless person died due to hypothermia. Temperatures across the area, were not considered to be extremely cold, as morning lows were in the 0 to 5 degree range, with wind chills dropping to -11 at KC International Airport.

An Arctic cold front moved rapidly southeast across southeast Missouri on the evening of the 11th. Rain changed to a brief period of freezing rain, then to sleet during the overnight hours. As Arctic air flooded in, the sleet changed to a brief period of snow before ending early in the morning on the 12th. Total accumulations of sleet and snow were from one-half to one inch in most areas. A small area of locally higher snow and sleet accumulations was east of a line from Sikeston to New Madrid, where accumulations were two to three inches. Across all areas, freezing rain produced a thin layer of glaze under the sleet and snow. Strong north winds frequently gusted to 35 mph, producing wind chills near zero at times. Very cold temperatures in the wake of the storm reduced the effectiveness of melting agents such as salt. Dangerous travel conditions persisted well after the ending of the precipitation. A few accidents with injuries were reported. On Highway 60 a couple miles east of Poplar Bluff, a man was moderately injured when his truck slid off the road into a bridge support. In New Madrid County, a motorist was seriously injured when his vehicle overturned in the median of Highway 60 near County Road 799.

Extreme cold temperatures was blamed for a death in Lebanon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme cold temperatures were responsible for a death of a 52 year old female walking home in Lebanon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of August 28 several thunderstorms affected the area with hail and wind. The winds ranged from 60 to 70 mph and did some damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. Hail sizes ranged from penny to half-dollar sized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: From KSHB 41 Action News: A 35-year-old Maryville, Missouri man died Tuesday afternoon after he was struck and killed by lightning while fishing. Maryville Public Safety officers were dispatched around 12:30 to the City Reservoir on a 9-1-1 call. When they arrived, they located Ryen Browning dead on the scene. An initial investigation indicated that Browning was fishing from the bank when he was struck by lightning.||Link to Article: https://www.kshb.com/news/state/missouri/missouri-man-struck-killed-by-lightning-while-fishing .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Chariton County reached or maintained D3 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D3 status maintenance. The direct impact to Chariton County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds gusted to between 40 and 50 mph during the afternoon hours. The winds shifted into the west-southwest at the same speeds following the passage of an occluded front during the early evening. The highest reported gust was 46 mph at the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff airports. A deep surface low pressure center moved east-northeast from the Kansas City area to northern Illinois. The strong winds occurred ahead of and behind a surface occluded front that arced southeast from the low. The presence of a mid-level dry slot allowed sufficient solar warming for the stronger winds to mix down to the surface in the afternoon.

Stock ponds continued to run low in some areas. The dry conditions contributed to an above normal potential for wildfires.

The strong winds occurred ahead of and behind a surface occluded front that arced southeast from the low. The presence of a mid-level dry slot allowed sufficient solar warming for the stronger winds to mix down to the surface in the afternoon.

Snow developed along an Arctic cold front as it sliced southeast across southeast Miissouri during the morning hours. As the front slowed down over the Tennessee Valley late in the day, a weak surface low developed on the front. This caused snow to persist along and south of U.S. Highway 60 through the evening hours. The result was an area of heavy snowfall from Doniphan east to Sikeston and Charleston southward, where 4 to 8 inches was reported. A few heavy snowfall reports included 8.5 inches at East Prairie (Mississippi County), 7 inches at Charleston, and 5 inches at Poplar Bluff. Across the remainder of southeast Missouri, including Cape Girardeau and Perryville, amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Visibility was frequently reduced below one mile in the heavy snowfall area. Much of the snow melted on treated road surfaces during the day, but conditions rapidly deteriorated after dark as temperatures dropped through the 20's. Actual air temperatures fell into the teens as the snow ended. These cold temperatures reduced the effectiveness of road salt, which slowed recovery efforts in the wake of the storm. Scattered traffic accidents were reported, including the Cape Girardeau area. The new snowfall on top of snow from the January 12th storm raised total snow depths to near a foot in the heavy snowfall area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist 500 mb shortwave trough tracked east from the Plains, causing a surface low pressure center to develop northeast from Texas to the central Appalachians. Widespread wintry precipitation occurred as colder air filtered south on the back side of the low. Around one-tenth of an inch of ice glazed surfaces across southeast Missouri. Near the onset of precipitation, a period of sleet was reported. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain. Thunder and lightning accompanied the wintry precipitation during the evening hours. Automated observing stations at the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff airports measured 0.13 and 0.08 inch of ice accretion, respectively. Most of the icing was on trees, power lines, and elevated surfaces during the precipitation. After the precipitation ended, temperatures fell through the 20's, causing icy spots to develop even on paved surfaces. On Interstate 57 northbound at the Mississippi River bridge, a vehicle slid into the bridge railing. One person was moderately injured. On U.S. Highway 60 in Stoddard County, one person was moderately injured when her vehicle overturned on the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over most of southeast Missouri, a mixture of freezing rain and sleet caused a light glaze of ice. Most of the icing was on elevated objects such as cars and decks. Some secondary roads and bridges were icy, resulting in several accidents. The most icing was reported north and west of Cape Girardeau, including Perry County. Several vehicles slid off secondary roads in Carter and Perry Counties. Main highways were treated with salt and mostly wet. The mixed precipitation was caused by a surface low pressure center that tracked northeast from Louisiana to the middle Ohio Valley.

Localized flash flooding and a couple of tornadoes occurred across southeast Missouri. A narrow swath of torrential rainfall occurred from Cape Girardeau southwest across Poplar Bluff, where water rescues were conducted. A surface low pressure center tracked rapidly north-northeast from Arkansas to the upper Mississippi Valley, dragging a warm front north across southeast Missouri. Instability was adequate for a couple tornadoes in the presence of very strong wind shear. A very moist air mass resulted in heavy rainfall, which produced localized flash flooding due to the water-logged ground. Most rivers experienced minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding developed on the Mississippi River. There was extensive flooding of agricultural land, however most of the cropland was dormant during the winter. Some low-lying roads were affected. The river continued rising into March.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front swept through the region during the afternoon and evening hours on January 15th. As cold air deepened snow developed across the region in response to a an upper level disturbance. Snow continued through the early morning hours on January 16th and tapered off by sunrise. Snowfall amounts ranged from 1 to 3 inches in Dunklin County and up to 5 inches in Pemiscot County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow.

Over most of southeast Missouri, a mixture of freezing rain and sleet caused a light glaze of ice. Most of the icing was on elevated objects such as cars and decks. Some secondary roads and bridges were icy, resulting in several accidents. The most icing was reported north and west of Cape Girardeau, including Perry County. Several vehicles slid off secondary roads in Carter and Perry Counties. Main highways were treated with salt and mostly wet. The mixed precipitation was caused by a surface low pressure center that tracked northeast from Louisiana to the middle Ohio Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist 500 mb shortwave trough tracked east from the Plains, causing a surface low pressure center to develop northeast from Texas to the central Appalachians. Widespread wintry precipitation occurred as colder air filtered south on the back side of the low. Around one-tenth of an inch of ice glazed surfaces across southeast Missouri. Near the onset of precipitation, a period of sleet was reported. Most of the precipitation fell in the form of freezing rain. Thunder and lightning accompanied the wintry precipitation during the evening hours. Automated observing stations at the Cape Girardeau and Poplar Bluff airports measured 0.13 and 0.08 inch of ice accretion, respectively. Most of the icing was on trees, power lines, and elevated surfaces during the precipitation. After the precipitation ended, temperatures fell through the 20's, causing icy spots to develop even on paved surfaces. On Interstate 57 northbound at the Mississippi River bridge, a vehicle slid into the bridge railing. One person was moderately injured. On U.S. Highway 60 in Stoddard County, one person was moderately injured when her vehicle overturned on the ice.

A line of developing showers and thunderstorms moved east across southeast Missouri. Locally strong winds accompanied this line of storms, including a 58 mph gust at the Cape Girardeau airport. The line of convection occurred along a cold front that trailed southwest from a surface low pressure center over lower Michigan.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The automated observing system at the Cape Girardeau airport measured a wind gust to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of heavy rain fell over the region leading up to February 24th. Storm total amounts ranged from 2 to 6 inches with the highest amounts along and south of I-44. Flash flooding was reported in a number of locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A number of rounds of rain moved through the region over several days. Thus the soils were saturated. Between one and three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Numerous roads were flooded including Highway D just southeast of Arcadia and Highway CC just southwest of Arcadia.

Localized flash flooding and a couple of tornadoes occurred across southeast Missouri. A narrow swath of torrential rainfall occurred from Cape Girardeau southwest across Poplar Bluff, where water rescues were conducted. A surface low pressure center tracked rapidly north-northeast from Arkansas to the upper Mississippi Valley, dragging a warm front north across southeast Missouri. Instability was adequate for a couple tornadoes in the presence of very strong wind shear. A very moist air mass resulted in heavy rainfall, which produced localized flash flooding due to the water-logged ground. Most rivers experienced minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over U.S. Highway 62 near Matthews.

Several rounds of widespread heavy rainfall tracked northeast across southeast Missouri. These rounds of heavy rain produced localized flooding of roads, as well as minor flooding of the St. Francis River. In the wake of a surface cold front, a persistent southwest flow of moist air aloft generated multiple rounds of widespread rain. The three-day rainfall at the Poplar Bluff airport was 4.18 inches for the 20th, 21st, and 22nd.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding of streets occurred in Poplar Bluff, where at least a couple of water rescues were conducted. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South of the confluence with the Ohio River, the Mississippi River remained well above flood stage following several rounds of heavy rain in late February. February monthly precipitation was 4 to 7 inches above normal, with monthly totals of 8 to 10 inches common. The river crested early in March, and then fell below flood stage around mid-month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the Mississippi River from late February. There was extensive flooding of agricultural land, however most of the cropland was dormant during the winter. Some low-lying roads were affected. At New Madrid, the river crested at 41.41 feet on the 5th. Flood stage there is 34 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: North of the confluence with the Ohio River, the Mississippi River fell below flood stage at Cape Girardeau early in the month. The flooding was minor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued on the Mississippi River from late February. The river backed up into several creeks and a diversion channel. Low-lying cropland was flooded, but it was mostly dormant. The river fell below flood stage very early in the month.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was damaged, a metal barn was leveled, and power lines were down on Highway 25. Debris was scattered approximately 300 yards. A pivot irrigation system was blown over.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two structures in town were struck by lightning. Both structures sustained damage from fires caused by the lightning strikes. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms developed near a warm front extending from central Missouri southeastward to the Tennessee Valley. The primary type of severe weather was large hail. However, local wind damage accompanied a small bowing segment that organized late in the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime-sized hail was reported at the junction of U.S. Highway 60 and State Route 21, and then east to Ellsinore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast along a warm front. The warm front extended southeast from a low pressure center between St. Louis and Springfield, Missouri. This activity produced a swath of large hail as well as isolated wind damage. The storms occurred in a weakly buoyant air mass, characterized by most-unstable capes around 1000 j/kg. Shear profiles were supportive of organization, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roughly 800 customers in the Cape Girardeau area were without power due to high winds, primarily west of Interstate 55.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving cold front drifted slowly southeast across southeast Missouri on the 27th, then stalled over the Tennessee Valley on the 28th. The front returned north as a warm front and stalled over the southeast corner of Missouri on the 29th. Widespread heavy rainfall from this oscillating front eventually resulted in isolated flooding of roads on the 29th. The heavy rain also caused minor flooding on the St. Francis River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car stalled in floodwaters on County Road 481 near where a creek crosses the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerous combination of heat and humidity overspread the region beginning on June 28th and persisted through July 1st. There were 37 heat related illnesses reported in St. Louis County & the City of St. Louis during this event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values 105 or higher each day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved southeast across the area, mainly along and east of Mississippi River. There were numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding and even a tornado with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of damaging winds moved southward across the City of St. Louis. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down. One large tree was blown down near the round-about heading into Forest Park near the zoo. Another large tree fell and was blocking Fountain Avenue. Just north of downtown a light pole was snapped off at the base.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved southeast across the area, mainly along and east of Mississippi River. There were numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding and even a tornado with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of damaging winds moved through northern portions of St. Louis County. Numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines were blown down in Bridgeton, Hazelwood, Florissant, Ferguson, Spanish Lake, and Glasgow Village. In Spanish Lake, a bus stop bench was blown into the street and a small shed was blown over. In Glasgow Village, a large tree was blown over onto a house, destroying it. Fortunately no one was home at the time and the family dogs were in the basement and unharmed. Another home in the area had a large portion of a tree fall on it, crashing through the roof and ceiling of the dining room and bedroom. The occupants  in the house made it to the basement just in time before the tree fell over. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along and southeast of a line from Cape Girardeau to Doniphan, strong gusty southwest winds occurred. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph at several locations. The peak gust measured at the Poplar Bluff airport was 49 mph. The Cape Girardeau airport measured a gust to 46 mph. The strong gusty winds were associated with a surface low pressure system that tracked east-southeast from Iowa to the upper Ohio Valley during the afternoon.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 tornado with a width of 100 yards and a track on the ground for 9 miles tore through portions of Wright County. The tornado had a maximum estimated wind speed of 130 mph. The track started near Macomb and lifted about 5 miles north of Norwood. One home was completely destroyed in additional to numerous trees and outbuildings blown down or damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures fell into the mid and upper 20's across southeast Missouri. The cold weather was accompanied by a coating of snow and some gusty winds. Fruit growers had expected that temperatures in the 20's would cause damage. Some nurseries at big box retailers had begun selling warm weather flowering plants and vegetables.  Many ornamental trees and shrubs were in flower except for dogwoods and azaleas.

South to southwest winds were sustained around 25 mph, with a few gusts around 50 mph. The highest measured wind gust was 56 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport. The Poplar Bluff airport gusted to 44 mph. The strong southwest winds occurred on the south side of a stationary front that extended from the Great Lakes region to Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe thunderstorms along a strong cold front produced numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail and one tornado during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3rd.

A slow moving cold front and a weak convergence zone just ahead of it were the focus of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. Storms trained across the region dumping up to three inches of rain in a short amount of time, especially across central portions of Ste. Francois county. Flash flooding was reported in Park Hills and Leadington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding in Park Hills and Leadington. One hundred feet of the Union Pacific lines had the soil washed out from under them near Main Street in Park Hills. Approximately 41 homes and 14 businesses sustained water damage. One of the homes suffered serious damage from the flash flooding. Sections of two city streets and one alley were destroyed. One car in Park Hills was swept away by the flood waters. Fortunately the occupants were able to make it out safely. At least a foot of water was rushing over U.S. Highway 67 in Leadington. No deaths or injuries were reported with this flash flood event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare April snowfall was observed in southeast Missouri. As surface low pressure moved from east Texas into Mississippi and Alabama, most precipitation on the afternoon and evening of April 6th stayed to the south. However, a secondary low developed and interacted with enough leftover moisture to produce a band of precipitation overnight into the morning hours on April 7th. Cold air funneled into the region through the night, allowing precipitation to quickly change over to snow, with a very brief transition period of sleet for some locations. Many locations picked up anywhere from 0.5 to 1 of snow, particularly in the southeast corner of the state. This was the 1st measurable snow in the month of April since 1983 for many locations. Impacts to travel were minimal due to warm pavement temperatures, which melted the snow on roadways. The highest reported snow amount was 1 inch in Sikeston and East Prairie, both south of Cape Girardeau.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized outbreak of severe thunderstorms occurred. Most of the severe weather reports were generated by two intense storm clusters. The first cluster crossed the Ozark foothills between Van Buren and Doniphan, then proceeded due east across Poplar Bluff and Dexter to the Mississippi River. This cluster occasionally acquired supercell characteristics. Large hail from 1 to 3 inches in diameter was common during most of its life cycle in southeast Missouri. A couple of tornadoes were spawned as well. A second less intense cluster moved east across southern Bollinger and Cape Girardeau Counties. This cluster produced hail up to the size of golf balls and an isolated wind gust up to near 60 mph. The severe storm outbreak occurred ahead of a  cold front that accelerated east-southeast toward the mid Mississippi Valley. Cooling aloft ahead of a shortwave trough over the Plains, combined with diurnal heating and moist southerly winds, resulted in a corridor of deepening convection ahead of the front. Steep mid-level lapse rates and strong effective wind shear promoted some very large hail within discrete storm cells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of severe thunderstorms along a strong cold front produced numerous reports of damaging winds and large hail and one tornado during the late afternoon and evening hours of April 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado developed near the intersection of County Roads 627 and 638 and moved east. The tornado damaged storage buildings, trees, power poles, and center pivot irrigation systems. A few residences also experienced shingle and roof damage. The tornado turned northeast after crossing State Highway K. The most significant damage was noted along County Road 657 south-southwest of Hornersville. The tornado dissipated in the Hornersville Swamp Conversation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of April 13 a large upper level system ejected into the plains and brought scattered thunderstorms to eastern Kansas and western Missouri. A few of these storms became supercells and produced quarter to golf ball sized hail. The largest stone reported this afternoon was 2 inches in the city of Olathe just on the other side of the Kansas/Missouri border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several buildings in Clarksdale, Missouri had their roofs blown off due to strong 70-plus mph winds. Emergency management indicated that there was no storm nearby, however post-event radar analysis indicated there may have been some echoes aloft that may have caused a very localized microburst. This entry reflects non-thunderstorm, due to the fact that there was no rain at the surface or lightning and thunder with these elevated echoes.

Gusty south to southwest winds shifted to a more westerly direction as a strong cold front associated with the low pressure system moved across the region. Peak wind gusts were measured up to 49 mph at Cape Girardeau. Most other peak wind gusts in southeast Missouri ranged from 40 to 47 mph.

Southwest winds increased to around 25 mph with peak gusts from 35 to around 45 mph. The highest reported gust was 46 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport. The strong winds occurred along and ahead of a cold front moving east across Missouri.
A complex of strong thunderstorms produced sub marginal hail and flash flooding across portions of the Missouri Bootheel during the overnight hours of August 16th into the 17th.
An MCV moved across northern Missouri and thunderstorms developed ahead of it. There were numerous reports of damaging winds with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A line of severe storms moved through Boone County. There were numerous reports of wind damage, especially in the northern half of the county. In Hallsville, a tree fell onto a transformer knocking out power to most of the town. On the north side of Columbia, a large section of a tree was blown down across the street from West Middle School. Three unoccupied vehicles sustained damage from the fallen tree. A mobile home sustained moderate roof damage in the 4200 block of Clark Lane, just east of the intersection of I-70 and U.S. Highway 63. Four miles north northeast of Murry, a 12 foot empty grain bin collapsed on one side.

Also, a farm outbuilding collapsed and there was some roof and porch damage to a nearby house. One mile northeast of Hatton, a new building collapsed at a business. Near Auxvasse, a tree was blown down onto County Road 262.

For the second consecutive morning, low temperatures fell below freezing. Lows ranged from the lower 20's in Wayne, Bollinger, and Perry Counties to the lower 30's from Poplar Bluff east to Sikeston and New Madrid. The back-to-back freezes adversely impacted fruit crops and some flowering plants and shrubs. The high pressure that brought the cold weather was centered over the middle and upper Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. The overall impact of the freezes was generally light. The peach and apple crops survived in good shape.

The largest winter weather event of the winter season occurred on Easter Sunday, April 1st, 2018.   The primary band of snow developed across northern Missouri and extended into west central Illinois during the late morning and afternoon hours.  Here 3 to 6 inches of snow was observed.  Further south along the I-70 corridor the precipitation was a mix of rain, sleet, snow and graupel.  Winter precipitation totals here ranged from a trace to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The largest winter weather event of the winter season occurred on Easter Sunday, April 1st, 2018.   The primary band of snow developed across northern Missouri and extended into west central Illinois between 1 pm and 7 pm.  In Lewis County between 6 and 7 inches of snow fell. Steffenville co-op reported 6.6 inches of snow while co-op observer in Canton reported 6 inches of snow.

Steep mid-level lapse rates and strong effective wind shear promoted some very large hail within discrete storm cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced a swath of large hail across the Dexter area. The hailstones ranged from the size of quarters to golf balls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized outbreak of severe thunderstorms occurred. Most of the severe weather reports were generated by two intense storm clusters. The first cluster crossed the Ozark foothills between Van Buren and Doniphan, then proceeded due east across Poplar Bluff and Dexter to the Mississippi River. This cluster occasionally acquired supercell characteristics. Large hail from 1 to 3 inches in diameter was common during most of its life cycle in southeast Missouri. A couple of tornadoes were spawned as well. A second less intense cluster moved east across southern Bollinger and Cape Girardeau Counties. This cluster produced hail up to the size of golf balls and an isolated wind gust up to near 60 mph. The severe storm outbreak occurred ahead of a  cold front that accelerated east-southeast toward the mid Mississippi Valley. Cooling aloft ahead of a shortwave trough over the Plains, combined with diurnal heating and moist southerly winds, resulted in a corridor of deepening convection ahead of the front. Steep mid-level lapse rates and strong effective wind shear promoted some very large hail within discrete storm cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down along Highway 153 and moved northeast toward the community of Baker. Several windows were broken. There was partial loss of metal roof covering. The top was broken off the chimney of a home. Two barns lost parts of their metal roofs, and walls were blown off. A garage sustained loss of shingles. A large entry door was blown in. Large limbs were broken off several trees. Several wooden power poles were partially blown over. Peak winds were estimated near 85 mph. The tornado lifted about one mile southeast of Baker. The last minute or two of the tornado was captured on video.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado occurred between Canalou and Matthews, north of State Highway H. A large farm implement building was destroyed. Parts of the building were lofted over one mile away. Four other barns and garages sustained damage, some of which was major. A grain bin was blown in on one side. At one residence, the roof of a rear porch was blown off, a window was broken, and large sections of shingles were torn off. A window was broken at another home. Farm implements were blown 20 to 50 feet. Power poles were down along Highway FF. Peak winds were estimated near 110 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive morning, low temperatures fell below freezing. Lows ranged from the lower 20's in Wayne, Bollinger, and Perry Counties to the lower 30's from Poplar Bluff east to Sikeston and New Madrid. The back-to-back freezes adversely impacted fruit crops and some flowering plants and shrubs. The high pressure that brought the cold weather was centered over the middle and upper Mississippi Valley early on the 8th. The overall impact of the freezes was generally light. The peach and apple crops survived in good shape.
As a warm front lifted north across the region, showers and storms developed. There were numerous reports of hail between three quarters of an inch and nickel size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of oppressive heat and humidity were experienced across southeast Missouri, where heat indices peaked in the triple digits on five consecutive days. The highest heat indices were observed on the 14th, when values from 110 to 115 were recorded along and south of a Sikeston to Poplar Bluff line. Areas north of Cape Girardeau including Perryville were impacted the least. At Perryville, heat indices peaked below 100 degrees on the 12th. Otherwise, heat index readings peaked from 100 to 109 each day at Perryville. High pressure over the Great Lakes region pushed a cold front down into the lower Ohio Valley. Heat indices remained persistently dangerous ahead of the cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged period of hazardous heat and humidity that began on June 29th persisted through the first several days of July. The most dangerous heat index values occurred on July 5th, when afternoon heat indices peaked around 110 degrees. Otherwise, the peak heat index was around 105 each day. Actual air temperatures were mostly in the lower to mid 90's, except for some upper 90's on the 5th. South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

Several days of oppressive heat and humidity were experienced across southeast Missouri, where heat indices peaked in the triple digits on five consecutive days. The highest heat indices were observed on the 14th, when values from 110 to 115 were recorded along and south of a Sikeston to Poplar Bluff line. Areas north of Cape Girardeau including Perryville were impacted the least. At Perryville, heat indices peaked below 100 degrees on the 12th. Otherwise, heat index readings peaked from 100 to 109 each day at Perryville. High pressure over the Great Lakes region pushed a cold front down into the lower Ohio Valley. Heat indices remained persistently dangerous ahead of the cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prolonged period of hazardous heat and humidity that began on June 29th persisted through the first several days of July. The most dangerous heat index values occurred on July 5th, when afternoon heat indices peaked around 110 degrees. Otherwise, the peak heat index was around 105 each day. Actual air temperatures were mostly in the lower to mid 90's, except for some upper 90's on the 5th. South winds ahead of a cold front brought heat and humidity on the 1st. The front stalled over the lower Ohio Valley on the 2nd, then returned northward. Another cold front approached on the 5th, preceded by hot and humid southwest winds.

Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jackson County reached or maintained D4 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D4 status maintenance. The direct impact to Jackson County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Putnam County reached or maintained D4 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D4 status maintenance. The direct impact to Putnam County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked east across Kansas, Missouri, and central Illinois on November 25th, bringing heavy snowfall and gusty winds to the region.  This caused blizzard conditions across portions of central and northeast Missouri, as well as west central Illinois, with less than a quarter of a mile visibility at times during the afternoon and evening. Strong northwest winds between 25 and 35 mph with gusts near 50 mph at times were reported during the storm. The heaviest snowfall reports were over portions of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Before the precipitation changed over to snow, there were a few strong to severe storms, but no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of snow fell across Knox County with winds gusting up to 45 mph at times. This caused reduced visibilities of less than a quarter of a mile at times and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values around 105 in the St. Louis metro area. Eight people were admitted to local hospitals for heat related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values around 105 in the St. Louis metro area. Four people were admitted to local hospitals for heat related illnesses in the City of St. Louis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms formed in the evening and night time hours on August 16. Large hail between 1 and 1.75 inch was reported, along with 60 to 70 mph winds. Most of the damage was relegated to tree, power line, and some minor structure damage.

Numerous thunderstorms formed in the evening and night time hours on August 16. Large hail between 1 and 1.75 inch was reported, along with 60 to 70 mph winds. Most of the damage was relegated to tree, power line, and some minor structure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail produced some damage to the siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front that extended from the Great Lakes region to southeast Missouri. The atmosphere was quite unstable, with surface-based capes near 3500 j/kg. An isolated storm produced a microburst.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst damaged a trucking business and brought down several tree limbs and a whole tree. A door and some windows of the trucking business were damaged. Power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms moved east-southeast across southeast Missouri during the evening. These storms produced large hail and an isolated damaging wind event. The storms occurred in a very unstable environment ahead of a cold front. This cold front extended from southern Indiana and Illinois into southeast Missouri and northern Arkansas. As the front shifted south, the main 500 mb shortwave over Missouri dove southeastward across the lower Mississippi Valley. Wind shear profiles favored organized storm structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage and power outages were reported throughout the community. Law enforcement estimated a wind gust to 60 mph just southeast of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm complex dropped east southeast out of Nebraska and produced some gusty winds near Maryville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 61 mph wind gust was measured near Maryville.

The amount of damages is unknown at this point, but numerous farmers were unable to get full return from their crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While much of the area saw some relief from the drought, many counties remained in D2-D4 status through the month of September. While the full scope of drought impacts are unknown, many farmers took losses on their hay and corn, opting to bale it for livestock or knock it down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms moved east-southeast across southeast Missouri during the evening. These storms produced large hail and an isolated damaging wind event. The storms occurred in a very unstable environment ahead of a cold front. This cold front extended from southern Indiana and Illinois into southeast Missouri and northern Arkansas. As the front shifted south, the main 500 mb shortwave over Missouri dove southeastward across the lower Mississippi Valley. Wind shear profiles favored organized storm structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported along U.S. Highway 60.

On the afternoon and evening of August 28 several thunderstorms affected the area with hail and wind. The winds ranged from 60 to 70 mph and did some damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. Hail sizes ranged from penny to half-dollar sized.

A broken line of thunderstorms moved slowly east across parts of southeast Missouri. One of these storms pulsed up to severe levels for a short time. The storms occurred along a weak cold front that trailed southwest from a low pressure center over southwest Ohio. A closed 500 mb low over east central Missouri provided additional support for the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail covered the ground in Aquilla. The biggest hailstones were estimated to be the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon and evening of August 28 several thunderstorms affected the area with hail and wind. The winds ranged from 60 to 70 mph and did some damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. Hail sizes ranged from penny to half-dollar sized.

On the afternoon and evening of August 28 several thunderstorms affected the area with hail and wind. The winds ranged from 60 to 70 mph and did some damage to trees, power lines, and some structures. Hail sizes ranged from penny to half-dollar sized.

From Cape Girardeau County south and west, widespread dense fog lowered visibility to one-quarter mile or less. Clear skies, calm winds, and damp ground from recent rains contributed to the dense fog. Surface high pressure was located directly over the lower Ohio Valley and extreme southeast Missouri.

Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Grundy County reached or maintained D4 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D4 status maintenance. The direct impact to Grundy County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes developed within a Quasi-Linear Convective System that moved across the region during the late evening of November 30th and continued into the early morning of the 1st.  The parent storm system intensified over the southern Plains and tracked northeast into northern Missouri.  Widespread severe wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph, hail and isolated tornadoes occurred. Seven tornadoes were confirmed over southwest and south central Missouri.  The tornadoes were rated EF-0 to EF-1 based on NWS Storm Surveys.  Unfortunately, there was one fatality that occurred in Aurora, Missouri when a tornado struck a local motel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A phone pole was blown down and partially over the road at West Dade 122 and North 115 in the Greenfield area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed north of a closed mid-level low over northern Mississippi and western Tennessee. During the warmest and most unstable time of day, one of the storms produced large hail. Surface-based capes peaked around 2,000 j/kg as the storms arrived. Many of the storms produced locally heavy rain, which caused several flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some standing water was over Highway 34 near the airport in Marble Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked east across Kansas, Missouri, and central Illinois on November 25th, bringing heavy snowfall and gusty winds to the region.  This caused blizzard conditions across portions of central and northeast Missouri, as well as west central Illinois, with less than a quarter of a mile visibility at times during the afternoon and evening. Strong northwest winds between 25 and 35 mph with gusts near 50 mph at times were reported during the storm. The heaviest snowfall reports were over portions of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Before the precipitation changed over to snow, there were a few strong to severe storms, but no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 5 inches of snow fell across Marion County with winds gusting up to 45 mph at times. This caused reduced visibilities of less than a quarter of a mile at times and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes developed within a Quasi-Linear Convective System that moved across the region during the late evening of November 30th and continued into the early morning of the 1st.  The parent storm system intensified over the southern Plains and tracked northeast into northern Missouri.  Widespread severe wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph, hail and isolated tornadoes occurred. Seven tornadoes were confirmed over southwest and south central Missouri.  The tornadoes were rated EF-0 to EF-1 based on NWS Storm Surveys.  Unfortunately, there was one fatality that occurred in Aurora, Missouri when a tornado struck a local motel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported estimated thunderstorm winds of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An outbreak of severe thunderstorms impacted the region on the last day of May. Damaging winds, large hail, and a tornado were observed in this outbreak. Scattered thunderstorm development began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. Scattered thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary across southeast Missouri and the lower Ohio Valley. Surface dewpoints near the boundary were in the lower 70s, which contributed to moderately strong instability. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the storms. Mid-level winds around 50 knots located just ahead of the shortwave helped to create moderate to strong deep-layer wind shear, favorable for a few supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The funnel cloud was reported by law enforcement northwest of town.

Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Saline County reached or maintained D3 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D3 status maintenance. The direct impact to Saline County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong thunderstorms developed ahead of a weak 500 mb trough over the Ozark Mountain region. The storms occurred in a moderately unstable air mass, with surface-based capes near 1,500 j/kg. One of the storms produced a non-supercell tornado, causing very minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A non-supercell tornado or landspout touched down along State Highway HH, causing some minor damage. The landspout occurred on the back side of a strengthening thunderstorm. The door was blown off the south side of a shop building and landed on the west side of the shop. The door caught an electrical service wire and tore it down. Very small tree limbs were down. Based on a storm survey from county storm spotters, this EF-0 tornado proceeded southeast to near the community of Qulin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed north of a closed mid-level low over northern Mississippi and western Tennessee. During the warmest and most unstable time of day, one of the storms produced large hail. Surface-based capes peaked around 2,000 j/kg as the storms arrived. Many of the storms produced locally heavy rain, which caused several flooding problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter-size hail fell just west of Sikeston.

The atmosphere in the vicinity of the front was moderately unstable. An influx of warm, moist air across the front supported intensification of storms north of the front. Due to rather weak wind shear, lines or clusters of storms quickly became outflow dominated. Isolated damaging wind gusts were reported with these outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was significant damage to a barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Along and ahead of a cold front moving eastward across southeast Missouri into southern Illinois, thunderstorms developed. Strong heating rapidly destabilized the air mass, with mixed-layer capes in excess of 3000 j/kg. Weak winds aloft limited the potential for sustained severe storms. A pulse-type severe storm produced isolated wind damage and hail.

An outbreak of severe thunderstorms impacted the region on the last day of May. Damaging winds, large hail, and a tornado were observed in this outbreak. Scattered thunderstorm development began by mid to late afternoon, with coverage increasing further into the evening hours. Scattered thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary across southeast Missouri and the lower Ohio Valley. Surface dewpoints near the boundary were in the lower 70s, which contributed to moderately strong instability. A subtle shortwave trough across the mid Mississippi Valley and lower Missouri Valley provided large-scale ascent for the storms. Mid-level winds around 50 knots located just ahead of the shortwave helped to create moderate to strong deep-layer wind shear, favorable for a few supercells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak winds were estimated near 90 mph. The tornado touched down on Highway V near County Road 607, then moved east across forested and hilly terrain. The tornado lifted as it crossed Highway 177 at the Trail of Tears State Park. Dozens of trees, some large, were broken or uprooted. Several power poles were snapped from fallen trees. The tornado was sighted by numerous people, and video was taken by at least one individual. The parent storm produced two small tornadoes on the other side of the Mississippi River near Wolf Lake, Illinois within the next 10 minutes.

Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes developed within a Quasi-Linear Convective System that moved across the region during the late evening of November 30th and continued into the early morning of the 1st.  The parent storm system intensified over the southern Plains and tracked northeast into northern Missouri.  Widespread severe wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph, hail and isolated tornadoes occurred. Seven tornadoes were confirmed over southwest and south central Missouri.  The tornadoes were rated EF-0 to EF-1 based on NWS Storm Surveys.  Unfortunately, there was one fatality that occurred in Aurora, Missouri when a tornado struck a local motel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down. No structural damage occurred.

Due to rather weak wind shear, lines or clusters of storms quickly became outflow dominated. Isolated damaging wind gusts were reported with these outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated winds gusted to 60 mph. There was also pea to marble size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system deepened over the Red River Valley on the 13th and tracked eastward into the Arklatex on the 14th before shifting northeastward toward Memphis on the 15th.  Several rounds of rain occurred during this time as moisture from the Gulf of Mexico streamed northward into the Ozarks. The highest rainfall totals occurred across the southeast portion of the region, where over two inches of rain fell. A localized report of flooding occurred from the heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Highway EE was closed due to flooding at the North Fork River low water crossing. This report was per the MODOT traveler map.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes developed within a Quasi-Linear Convective System that moved across the region during the late evening of November 30th and continued into the early morning of the 1st.  The parent storm system intensified over the southern Plains and tracked northeast into northern Missouri.  Widespread severe wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph, hail and isolated tornadoes occurred. Seven tornadoes were confirmed over southwest and south central Missouri.  The tornadoes were rated EF-0 to EF-1 based on NWS Storm Surveys.  Unfortunately, there was one fatality that occurred in Aurora, Missouri when a tornado struck a local motel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two large trees were uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked east across Kansas, Missouri, and central Illinois on November 25th, bringing heavy snowfall and gusty winds to the region.  This caused blizzard conditions across portions of central and northeast Missouri, as well as west central Illinois, with less than a quarter of a mile visibility at times during the afternoon and evening. Strong northwest winds between 25 and 35 mph with gusts near 50 mph at times were reported during the storm. The heaviest snowfall reports were over portions of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Before the precipitation changed over to snow, there were a few strong to severe storms, but no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 6 inches of snow fell across Shelby County with winds gusting up to 45 mph at times. This caused reduced visibilities of less than a quarter of a mile at times and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 28-30���Showers and storms developed ahead of a cold front that moved from eastern Kansas into central and southern Missouri on the 28th and 29th, with additional storm Images show rainfall accumulations development on the 30th as the front shifted back to the north of the Ozarks. The heaviest rainfall occurred during the morning and afternoon of the 30th, as a mesoscale convective system that developed near Wichita during the early morning of the 30th moved slowly eastward into southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri. Repeated development of storms over the region led to rainfall accumulations in excess of four inches over portions of Stone, Christian, Cherokee and Bourbon Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding were received in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The city Emergency Management reported a rain gauge in north Nixa recorded 3.60 inches of rain fall with a maximum hourly rain rate of 0.85 inch per hour.  There were multiple reports of flooded roadways in the Nixa, including Tracker Road near Raspberry Court, Phillips Road between Tracker Road and Guin Road, and Tracker Road near Sanders Valley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerous combination of heat and humidity overspread the region beginning on June 28th and persisted through July 1st. There were 37 heat related illnesses reported in St. Louis County & the City of St. Louis during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values 105 or higher each day. There were 37 heat-related illness for the 4 day excessive heat event in St. Louis County and the City of St. Louis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective complex moved southeast across the area, mainly along and east of Mississippi River. There were numerous reports of damaging winds, large hail, flash flooding and even a tornado with some of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wide swath of damaging winds moved across the eastern half of St. Charles County. There were numerous reports of large trees, tree branches and power lines blown down. In Portage Des Sioux at two local marinas on the Mississippi River, several boats were cast into the main channel. Also, a dock was flipped over as well as a pickup truck. Near West Alton, a wooden barn was flattened and several large trees blown down. Winds were estimated between 60 and 80 MPH in the Portage Des Sioux area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Schuyler County reached or maintained D3 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D3 status maintenance. The direct impact to Schuyler County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor river flooding occurred as a result of a stretch of wet weather during the early part of the month. Average rainfall amounts between the 5th and 10th were between 3 and 4 inches east of the Mississippi River, and mainly 1 to 2 inches west of the river. Much of this rain was due to the remnants of Tropical Storm Gordon, which got caught up with a cold front moving through the central states.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Mississippi River. The river backed up into several creeks. Some low-lying agricultural land was inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms developed across the region north of a warm front and along various outflow boundaries. Some of the storms produced large hail and damaging winds. In south St. Louis County, a 70 year old woman died from the heat in her home on June 14th. Her air conditioning had broken the day before and she had setup a repair appointment for the day she died. Temperatures/heat index values on the 13th and 14th did not meet either heat advisory or heat warning criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several large tree limbs on the west side of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 19-20���a rapidly deepening low pressure system tracked from south central Kansas to southwest Iowa. Unseasonably strong wind shear led to the development of severe storms with heavy rainfall across southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 19th . Several EF-0 and two EF-1 tornadoes developed with these storms over Barton, Vernon, Barry and Webster Counties. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one half inch and one inch, with isolated amounts up to three inches over Wright and Barton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low water crossings in and around the city of West Plains were flooded and roads were barricaded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While much of the area saw some relief from the drought, many counties remained in D2-D4 status through the month of September. While the full scope of drought impacts are unknown, many farmers took losses on their hay and corn, opting to bale it for livestock or knock it down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The drought of 2018 continued for Harrison County, however an influx of some moisture brought some minor relief to the county. Conditions improved from D3 to D2 during the month of September, but the impacts and losses of several crops were already felt across the region. The amount of damages is unknown at this point, but numerous farmers were unable to get full return from their crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 19-20���a rapidly deepening low pressure system tracked from south central Kansas to southwest Iowa. Unseasonably strong wind shear led to the development of severe storms with heavy rainfall across southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 19th . Several EF-0 and two EF-1 tornadoes developed with these storms over Barton, Vernon, Barry and Webster Counties. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one half inch and one inch, with isolated amounts up to three inches over Wright and Barton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 24 hour rainfall total of 2.32 inches was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sullivan County reached or maintained D4 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D4 status maintenance. The direct impact to Sullivan County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 19-20���a rapidly deepening low pressure system tracked from south central Kansas to southwest Iowa. Unseasonably strong wind shear led to the development of severe storms with heavy rainfall across southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 19th . Several EF-0 and two EF-1 tornadoes developed with these storms over Barton, Vernon, Barry and Webster Counties. Rainfall accumulations averaged between one half inch and one inch, with isolated amounts up to three inches over Wright and Barton Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF1 tornado touched down near Highway Z and 39 in Barry County and continued north northeast for approximately 3.4 miles before crossing into Lawrence County ending west of Marionville. Maximum wind speeds were estimated at 90mph, a width 100 yards and total distance 9.4 miles. Numerous outbuildings were damaged along the path, trees were uprooted, some power lines were downed, and two mobile homes had portions of their roofs removed. The tornado initially touched down at 6:36 pm in Barry County and ended lifted at 7:01 pm in Lawrence County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 4 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding across the central portions of Boone County, including the Columbia. Numerous roads were flooded including Interstate 70 near mile marker 131 in the eastbound lanes. A high water rescue was performed on East St. Charles Road near intersection with Battle Avenue on the northeast side of Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 28-30���Showers and storms developed ahead of a cold front that moved from eastern Kansas into central and southern Missouri on the 28th and 29th, with additional storm Images show rainfall accumulations development on the 30th as the front shifted back to the north of the Ozarks. The heaviest rainfall occurred during the morning and afternoon of the 30th, as a mesoscale convective system that developed near Wichita during the early morning of the 30th moved slowly eastward into southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri. Repeated development of storms over the region led to rainfall accumulations in excess of four inches over portions of Stone, Christian, Cherokee and Bourbon Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding were received in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an antenna on a house five miles NNW of Hartville. Minor damage was reported. The time of the strike was estimated from radar and lightning plots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerous combination of heat and humidity overspread the region beginning on June 28th and persisted through July 1st. There were 37 heat related illnesses reported in St. Louis County & the City of St. Louis during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values 105 or higher each day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms with heavy rain occurred along a weak stationary front that extended from central Arkansas northeast to southern Illinois. The storms slowed down or stalled near the front, causing isolated flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over a couple of main roads, including Highway 51 south of Perryville and Highway T in several spots between Perryville and Silver Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe pulse storms occurred across the area on an afternoon basis from August 7-11. The most impactful afternoon/evening occurred on August 10 when severe pulse storms affected the Springfield metropolitan area. The Route 66 Festival with estimated 60,000 people was in both a Severe Thunderstorm and Flash Flood Warning with eventual cancellation of activities for the night. On August 7-8���A storm complex that developed during the late evening of the 6th and early morning hours of the 7th over Kansas pushed an outflow boundary into southwest Missouri.||Strong to severe storms developed along the boundary intermittently from the late morning of the 7th to the late afternoon of the 8th . The strongest storms impacted areas to the south and east of Springfield, and produced straight line wind damage to trees and roofs. One barn in Oregon County was destroyed due to the strong winds and a boat dock on Table Rock Lake was flipped over. Several roads in Branson flooded as a result of heavy rainfall during the afternoon of the 8th. Two-day rainfall totals averaged between one and one and a half inches along and south of Highway 60. Localized accumulations over two inches occurred near Branson. From August 9-12, a weak front stalled over the region and interacted with a very moist and unstable air mass to produce scattered late afternoon and early evening strong to severe storms across the region each day. Several of the storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls, wind damage to trees, powerlines, boat docks and roofs, and localized flash flooding. The majority of the storms affected the western half of the region on the 9th and 10th , with storm coverage becoming more isolated on the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of quarter size hail was received via social media. The time was estimated by radar.
