On August 7-8���A storm complex that developed during the late evening of the 6th and early morning hours of the 7th over Kansas pushed an outflow boundary into southwest Missouri.||Strong to severe storms developed along the boundary intermittently from the late morning of the 7th to the late afternoon of the 8th . The strongest storms impacted areas to the south and east of Springfield, and produced straight line wind damage to trees and roofs. One barn in Oregon County was destroyed due to the strong winds and a boat dock on Table Rock Lake was flipped over. Several roads in Branson flooded as a result of heavy rainfall during the afternoon of the 8th. Two-day rainfall totals averaged between one and one and a half inches along and south of Highway 60. Localized accumulations over two inches occurred near Branson. From August 9-12, a weak front stalled over the region and interacted with a very moist and unstable air mass to produce scattered late afternoon and early evening strong to severe storms across the region each day. Several of the storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls, wind damage to trees, powerlines, boat docks and roofs, and localized flash flooding. The majority of the storms affected the western half of the region on the 9th and 10th , with storm coverage becoming more isolated on the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Automated Weather Observing System located in Branson West recorded a 61mph wind gust.

On August 7-8���A storm complex that developed during the late evening of the 6th and early morning hours of the 7th over Kansas pushed an outflow boundary into southwest Missouri.||Strong to severe storms developed along the boundary intermittently from the late morning of the 7th to the late afternoon of the 8th . The strongest storms impacted areas to the south and east of Springfield, and produced straight line wind damage to trees and roofs. One barn in Oregon County was destroyed due to the strong winds and a boat dock on Table Rock Lake was flipped over. Several roads in Branson flooded as a result of heavy rainfall during the afternoon of the 8th. Two-day rainfall totals averaged between one and one and a half inches along and south of Highway 60. Localized accumulations over two inches occurred near Branson. From August 9-12, a weak front stalled over the region and interacted with a very moist and unstable air mass to produce scattered late afternoon and early evening strong to severe storms across the region each day. Several of the storms produced hail up to the size of golf balls, wind damage to trees, powerlines, boat docks and roofs, and localized flash flooding. The majority of the storms affected the western half of the region on the 9th and 10th , with storm coverage becoming more isolated on the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed just west of the intersection of Highways 123 and 13, southwest of Humansville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 16���a meso-scale convective complex that developed over eastern Kansas during the afternoon tracked eastward across southeast Kansas and the Missouri Ozarks during the late afternoon and evening hours, producing strong to severe storms with heavy rainfall. Hail up to 2.5 inches in diameter was reported near Granby in Newton County along with sporadic straight-line wind damage elsewhere.  Heavy rain was also reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported tennis ball size hail via social media just south of Granby, Mo.  The hail fell in a rural area and produces little damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: August 28-30���Showers and storms developed ahead of a cold front that moved from eastern Kansas into central and southern Missouri on the 28th and 29th, with additional storm Images show rainfall accumulations development on the 30th as the front shifted back to the north of the Ozarks. The heaviest rainfall occurred during the morning and afternoon of the 30th, as a mesoscale convective system that developed near Wichita during the early morning of the 30th moved slowly eastward into southeast Kansas and southwest Missouri. Repeated development of storms over the region led to rainfall accumulations in excess of four inches over portions of Stone, Christian, Cherokee and Bourbon Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding were received in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Cooperative Observer reported that 2.42 inches of rain fell in approximately one and a half hours.

Repeated development of storms over the region led to rainfall accumulations in excess of four inches over portions of Stone, Christian, Cherokee and Bourbon Counties. Numerous reports of flash flooding were received in these areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A HAM Operator by the Springfield Nature Center reported a rail total of 2.50 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms moved east across the Ozark Mountains into the Current River Valley. One of the strongest storms along the leading edge of the complex downed some trees. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that was advancing south-southeast across the Missouri Valley. These storms were near the southern periphery of a mid-level closed low digging across the middle Missouri Valley, where 30 to 40 knot westerly 500 mb flow contributed to moderately strong wind shear.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in east central Missouri during the afternoon along a remnant outflow boundary from the morning thunderstorms. The afternoon storms moved southeast along and ahead of the slowing outflow boundary. The storms intensified in the unstable air south of the boundary. Afternoon heating contributed to moderately strong instability. Rather strong wind shear resulted in some organized lines and bowing lines of storms. Sporadic damaging winds occurred in association with the lines. An isolated supercell with large hail occurred in the Ozark foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported a funnel cloud.

A woman had driven into flood waters on Colony Church Road and became stuck. No one sustained injuries in either incident.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm complex raced southward across southern Illinois and western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Widespread wind damage occurred with this long-lived complex, which met the definition of a derecho. Southeast Missouri was on the western flank of the complex, where wind damage was limited to a few isolated reports. This convective system took shape over the Missouri/Iowa border region and then progressed south-southeast to the Tennessee Valley by late evening. A very moist and unstable air mass set the stage for this derecho. Surface dew points were near 80 degrees, with mixed-layer capes over 4000 j/kg. When the atmosphere is that unstable, wind shear does not need to be particularly strong for significant severe weather to occur. The bulk wind shear in the 0 to 6 km layer averaged 20 to 30 knots. Deep northerly wind flow averaged around 20 knots from 850 to 500 mb on the front side of a high pressure ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a wind gust to 63 mph at the intersection of State Route 51 and County Road 470.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A loosely organized band of thunderstorms developed over southeast Missouri during the heat of the afternoon, and then moved southeast. The strongest storm cells within this band produced strong gusty winds, including a couple of damaging wind events. A surface front was reinforced and pushed south near the Ohio River from overnight and morning convection. The afternoon storms developed along this boundary stretching from eastern Kentucky back to southeastern Missouri. Weak winds aloft kept the activity rather limited in terms of intensity and organization. However, some storms were capable of rainfall rates from 1.5 to 2.5 inches per hour. These storms occurred in an area of enhanced southwesterly 850 mb flow from 20 to 30 knots, where precipitable water values were near 1.7 inches. Isolated flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down, causing several power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large cluster of thunderstorms moved south-southeast down the Mississippi River Valley during the evening hours. The cluster intensified to severe levels for a short time as it passed through southeast Missouri. The persistent cluster of storms moved into a very favorable air mass over southeast Missouri, with dew points in the mid to upper 70's and strong instability. The storms produced locally damaging winds. Low wind shear inhibited the formation of a longer-lived, more organized severe event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down, along with minor damage to a few buildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm complex raced southward across southern Illinois and western Kentucky during the early evening hours. Widespread wind damage occurred with this long-lived complex, which met the definition of a derecho. Southeast Missouri was on the western flank of the complex, where wind damage was limited to a few isolated reports. This convective system took shape over the Missouri/Iowa border region and then progressed south-southeast to the Tennessee Valley by late evening. A very moist and unstable air mass set the stage for this derecho. Surface dew points were near 80 degrees, with mixed-layer capes over 4000 j/kg. When the atmosphere is that unstable, wind shear does not need to be particularly strong for significant severe weather to occur. The bulk wind shear in the 0 to 6 km layer averaged 20 to 30 knots. Deep northerly wind flow averaged around 20 knots from 850 to 500 mb on the front side of a high pressure ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A person was struck and injured by a flying awning at the intersection of Highway 51 and U.S. Highway 61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of numerous thunderstorms during the mid-afternoon hours, the atmosphere destabilized enough for an isolated storm to develop early in the evening. This storm produced a weak tornado that was well-documented on video. The storm occurred along an eastward-moving cold front. The environment near the front remained very moist and moderately unstable, supportive of intense convection. Within the exit region of a 50 to 60 kt 500 mb jet, strengthening bulk wind shear supported storm organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief funnel cloud was observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the passage of numerous thunderstorms during the mid-afternoon hours, the atmosphere destabilized enough for an isolated storm to develop early in the evening. This storm produced a weak tornado that was well-documented on video. The storm occurred along an eastward-moving cold front. The environment near the front remained very moist and moderately unstable, supportive of intense convection. Within the exit region of a 50 to 60 kt 500 mb jet, strengthening bulk wind shear supported storm organization.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the tornado track was over burnt wheat field and dirt farm fields. No structures or trees were hit. The path length was determined mainly by photos and video submitted to a local television station, since there was no damage path evident. The photos and video showed the tornado was likely moving slower than the parent storm. The tornado formed just east of Highway 153, then tracked east along Route A for several miles. The tornado ended along Route A as it reached the New Madrid County line. State police officers reported the tornado skipped intermittently along the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed in the heat of the afternoon south of an outflow boundary that was draped across central Illinois and east central Missouri. Vertical wind shear was very weak, resulting in little if any storm organization. Even so, a few storms briefly pulsed up to severe levels. Damaging wind gusts were very isolated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very localized wind damage occurred. A large tree was uprooted and fell on a home, causing minor damage to the home and a vehicle. One side of the house had damaged shingles from high wind. A carport was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms formed in east central Missouri during the afternoon along a remnant outflow boundary from the morning thunderstorms. The afternoon storms moved southeast along and ahead of the slowing outflow boundary. The storms intensified in the unstable air south of the boundary. Afternoon heating contributed to moderately strong instability. Rather strong wind shear resulted in some organized lines and bowing lines of storms. Sporadic damaging winds occurred in association with the lines. An isolated supercell with large hail occurred in the Ozark foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A concrete storage shed was heavily damaged in Dexter. Bleachers were blown over at a ball field in Bernie.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon and evening hours of July 14th. Numerous reports of damaging winds were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down numerous trees and tree limbs between Florissant and Dellwood. One of the trees fell onto a house on South Hartnett Avenue causing moderate roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of June 26 a line of storms pushed through the Kansas City Metro area. As these storms exited the metro they produced a tornado that impacted the Lake Lotawana area. The extent of the damage was to trees, as well as to vehicles and lakeside docks and boats. No one was injured by the EF-0 tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southwest winds occurred ahead of a cold front that was dropping southward across Missouri and Illinois. Peak wind gusts were mostly from 40 to 50 mph. The highest measured gust at any airport sites was 48 mph at the Cape Girardeau airport.

Unseasonably hot and humid weather settled over Missouri during the Fourth of July holiday period. Highs were in the mid 90s with heat index values between 100 and 110. As a result, at least 100 people suffered heat-related illnesses at various outdoor events. Of these, approximately 35 people had to be taken to local hospitals for further treatment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot and humid weather settled over Missouri during the Fourth of July holiday, July 4th and 5th. High temperatures were in the mid 90s both days with heat index values between 100 and 110. As a result, at least 50 people had to be treated for heat illnesses at Fair St. Louis, while approximately 20 people where taken to local hospitals for further treatment.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Thursday, July 19, significant severe weather occurred in the EAX CWA in two distinct but consecutive rounds. The first round was a line of supercells that formed over the eastern Kansas City metro, produced significant hail up to baseball size, and drifted south southeast with time; the second was an MCS dropping southeast out of the TOP CWA and producing straight-line winds across southwestern portions of the EAX CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ping pong ball sized hail covered the deck like snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The anomalously dry period that plagued the region during the summer of 2018 continued into and through July. Most areas were about 2 inches short of normal precipitation for the month of July. Most of northern Missouri, north of the Missouri River, came up between 4 and 5 inches short of normal. This combined with the dry June has caused the drought across the region to worsen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The abnormally dry summer continued into and through July for Clinton County. The Drought Monitor put the county in D3 and maintained it into August. As of yet, the breadth and magnitude of the impacts are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked east across Kansas, Missouri, and central Illinois on November 25th, bringing heavy snowfall and gusty winds to the region.  This caused blizzard conditions across portions of central and northeast Missouri, as well as west central Illinois, with less than a quarter of a mile visibility at times during the afternoon and evening. Strong northwest winds between 25 and 35 mph with gusts near 50 mph at times were reported during the storm. The heaviest snowfall reports were over portions of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Before the precipitation changed over to snow, there were a few strong to severe storms, but no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 2 inches of snow fell across Pike County with winds gusting up to 40 mph at times. This caused reduced visibilities of less than a quarter of a mile at times and hazardous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting on October 6th, the atmosphere set itself up in a pattern conducive to bring a prolonged period of heavy rain to portions of northeastern Kansas and northwestern Missouri. The heaviest rain occurred Saturday and Sunday (Oct 6-7). By Tuesday night (October 9th) more than 11 inches of rain fell in and around the Kansas City Metro. Local drought conditions were still in the Extreme to Exceptional category going into the event, so flash flooding was relatively low impact, relegated mostly to several road closures. The bigger impact from this prolonged rainfall event came from local river and stream flooding. Several streams entered the moderate to major flooding category. The 4 day event spanning Oct 6-9 resulted in the greatest 4 day rain total in recorded Kansas City History. At Kansas City International Airport 9.79 inches were recorded for the Oct 6-9 time period, beating out Sept 12-15, 1977, which had 9.48 over the 4 day span.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A school bus was stranded at Oldham  Road and Hillcrest Road due to flooding over the roadway. The driver and all 20 students onboard were able to evacuated safely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very dry summer, exceptional drought (D4) conditions were experienced area-wide, resulting in heavy losses for local farmers. Things changed in October when widespread heavy rain effectively ended that drought. Widespread 6 to 9 inches of rain fell, with some locations receiving over a foot of rain over the 4 day stretch from October 6 through October 9. By October 9th, the drought was effectively ended by the UNL drought monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to widespread dry conditions through the summer and early fall of 2018 most counties experienced extreme to exceptional drought (D3-D4). While some counties saw marked improvement through the late summer and early fall the drought continued into the second week of October. The drought improved area-wide after 6-12 inches of rain fell in a four day stretch in early October. This effectively ended the drought area-wide. While the exact damage costs are unknown, it is estimated that farmer across the entire region suffered millions of dollars of losses due to the extremely dry conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very dry summer, exceptional drought (D4) conditions were experienced area-wide, resulting in heavy losses for local farmers. Things changed in October when widespread heavy rain effectively ended that drought. Widespread 6 to 9 inches of rain fell, with some locations receiving over a foot of rain over the 4 day stretch from October 6 through October 9. By October 9th, the drought was effectively ended by the UNL drought monitor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The anomalously dry period that plagued the region during the summer of 2018 continued into and through July. Most areas were about 2 inches short of normal precipitation for the month of July. Most of northern Missouri, north of the Missouri River, came up between 4 and 5 inches short of normal. This combined with the dry June has caused the drought across the region to worsen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The abnormally dry summer continued into and through July for Grundy County. The Drought Monitor put the county in D3 and maintained it into August. As of yet, the breadth and magnitude of the impacts are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buchanan County reached or maintained D2 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D2 status maintenance. The direct impact to Buchanan County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southern stream storm system taking nearly the same track as a previous system on  December 26-27 brought widespread rainfall amounts of over an inch from the evening of the 30th to the late morning of the 31st. Due to saturated soil conditions before this round of rain, additional flooding of low water crossings occurred, mainly north and east of Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low water crossing on Maries Road 624 just north of Dixon was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure tracked east across Kansas, Missouri, and central Illinois on November 25th, bringing heavy snowfall and gusty winds to the region.  This caused blizzard conditions across portions of central and northeast Missouri, as well as west central Illinois, with less than a quarter of a mile visibility at times during the afternoon and evening. Strong northwest winds between 25 and 35 mph with gusts near 50 mph at times were reported during the storm. The heaviest snowfall reports were over portions of northeast Missouri and west central Illinois. Before the precipitation changed over to snow, there were a few strong to severe storms, but no reports of severe weather were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 2 and 4 inches of snow fell across Ralls County with winds gusting up to 45 mph at times. This caused reduced visibilities of less than a quarter of a mile at times and hazardous driving conditions.

The anomalously dry period that plagued the region during the summer of 2018 continued into and through July. Most areas were about 2 inches short of normal precipitation for the month of July. Most of northern Missouri, north of the Missouri River, came up between 4 and 5 inches short of normal. This combined with the dry June has caused the drought across the region to worsen.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The abnormally dry summer continued into and through July for Howard County. The Drought Monitor put the county in D3 and maintained it into August. As of yet, the breadth and magnitude of the impacts are unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level cold air filtered into the region while low pressure tracked along the Gulf Coast. Warm air lifting over the cold airmass resulted in precipitation across the Mid-South. Across the Bootheel temperatures dropped below freezing resulting in freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to two tenths inch of ice accumulated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around one tenth inch of ice accumulated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure moved southeast from the northern Plains into the lower Ohio Valley. Clear skies and light winds set the stage for widespread frost and a brief period of sub-freezing temperatures. Low temperatures ranged from the mid 20's in the hilly terrain northwest of Cape Girardeau to near 32 in the Arkansas border counties. The growing season was ended by this first freeze of the season.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes developed within a Quasi-Linear Convective System that moved across the region during the late evening of November 30th and continued into the early morning of the 1st.  The parent storm system intensified over the southern Plains and tracked northeast into northern Missouri.  Widespread severe wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph, hail and isolated tornadoes occurred. Seven tornadoes were confirmed over southwest and south central Missouri.  The tornadoes were rated EF-0 to EF-1 based on NWS Storm Surveys.  Unfortunately, there was one fatality that occurred in Aurora, Missouri when a tornado struck a local motel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fair Grove Police Department utility pole, with attached city infrastructure equipment, snapped in half at 70 East Willow Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree down on a house near Burlington Junction.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very dry summer, exceptional drought (D4) conditions were experienced area-wide, resulting in heavy losses for local farmers. Things changed in October when widespread heavy rain effectively ended that drought. Widespread 6 to 9 inches of rain fell, with some locations receiving over a foot of rain over the 4 day stretch from October 6 through October 9. By October 9th, the drought was effectively ended by the UNL drought monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to widespread dry conditions through the summer and early fall of 2018 most counties experienced extreme to exceptional drought (D3-D4). While some counties saw marked improvement through the late summer and early fall the drought continued into the second week of October. The drought improved area-wide after 6-12 inches of rain fell in a four day stretch in early October. This effectively ended the drought area-wide. While the exact damage costs are unknown, it is estimated that farmer across the entire region suffered millions of dollars of losses due to the extremely dry conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Atchison County reached or maintained D2 drought status through at least August 21, when the county was removed from the D2 status by the Drought Monitor. The direct impact to Atchison County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A southern stream storm system taking nearly the same track as a previous system on  December 26-27 brought widespread rainfall amounts of over an inch from the evening of the 30th to the late morning of the 31st. Due to saturated soil conditions before this round of rain, additional flooding of low water crossings occurred, mainly north and east of Springfield.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive rainfall caused a section of Highway D to flood and become impassable to motorists. This area is simply a head water depression and not a named creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold high pressure moved southeast from the northern Plains into the lower Ohio Valley. Clear skies and light winds set the stage for widespread frost and a brief period of sub-freezing temperatures. Low temperatures ranged from the mid 20's in the hilly terrain northwest of Cape Girardeau to near 32 in the Arkansas border counties. The growing season was ended by this first freeze of the season.

Starting on October 6th, the atmosphere set itself up in a pattern conducive to bring a prolonged period of heavy rain to portions of northeastern Kansas and northwestern Missouri. The heaviest rain occurred Saturday and Sunday (Oct 6-7). By Tuesday night (October 9th) more than 11 inches of rain fell in and around the Kansas City Metro. Local drought conditions were still in the Extreme to Exceptional category going into the event, so flash flooding was relatively low impact, relegated mostly to several road closures. The bigger impact from this prolonged rainfall event came from local river and stream flooding. Several streams entered the moderate to major flooding category. The 4 day event spanning Oct 6-9 resulted in the greatest 4 day rain total in recorded Kansas City History. At Kansas City International Airport 9.79 inches were recorded for the Oct 6-9 time period, beating out Sept 12-15, 1977, which had 9.48 over the 4 day span.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were flooded in Sugar Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of Thursday, July 19, significant severe weather occurred in the EAX CWA in two distinct but consecutive rounds. The first round was a line of supercells that formed over the eastern Kansas City metro, produced significant hail up to baseball size, and drifted south southeast with time; the second was an MCS dropping southeast out of the TOP CWA and producing straight-line winds across southwestern portions of the EAX CWA.

The drought improved area-wide after 6-12 inches of rain fell in a four day stretch in early October. This effectively ended the drought area-wide. While the exact damage costs are unknown, it is estimated that farmer across the entire region suffered millions of dollars of losses due to the extremely dry conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system lifted northeast across the bootheel of Missouri into the Ohio Valley. North of the system, a strong deformation zone set up with a swath of heavier snowfall from east central Missouri into southwestern Illinois. By the time the snow came to an end during the afternoon hours of November 15th, up to 9 inches of snow fell with the highest amounts over portions of Warren and St. Charles counties in Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell across St. Charles County. The heaviest snow fell in the Wentzville and Foristell areas with 8.7 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe storms developed along an old outflow boundary over portions of central Missouri. Some of the storms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees and numerous tree limbs around Eolia. One tree fell onto a house causing moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level high pressure dominated the region during the middle of July 2018. High temperatures and humidity combined to create dangerous heat indices across the Mid-South.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index values exceeded 105 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of powerful thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front moving southeast from Iowa into far northeast Missouri during the evening hours of August 28th. The line of thunderstorms produced damaging winds in Clark County.  Very heavy rainfall was also reported with this storm with reports of 3 to  4 inches in far northeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that winds were mostly around 40 MPH but reported a brief gust of 60 MPH.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe storms moved through the St. Louis metro area. Some of the storms produced large hail and flash flooding.
Isolated severe storms moved through the St. Louis metro area. Some of the storms produced large hail and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to three inches of rain fell in a short amount of time causing flash flooding. Interstate 70 near St. Louis Lambert International Airport was briefly closed due to water flowing over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values around 105 in the St. Louis metro area for four straight days. Therefore the heat advisory was upgraded to an Excessive Heat Warning during the early morning hours of August 26th. Seven people were admitted to local hospitals for heat related illnesses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High temperatures were in the mid 90s with heat index values around 105 in the St. Louis metro area. Four people were admitted to local hospitals for heat related illnesses in St. Louis County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the upper Mississippi River basin caused a rise in the river level about mid-month. Minor flooding was observed in some places.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred along the Mississippi River. Some low-lying farmland near the river was inundated. The flood gate on Themis Street in Cape Girardeau was closed. The Mississippi River backed into several creeks, producing flooding well away from the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized flash flooding and a couple of tornadoes occurred across southeast Missouri. A narrow swath of torrential rainfall occurred from Cape Girardeau southwest across Poplar Bluff, where water rescues were conducted. A surface low pressure center tracked rapidly north-northeast from Arkansas to the upper Mississippi Valley, dragging a warm front north across southeast Missouri. Instability was adequate for a couple tornadoes in the presence of very strong wind shear. A very moist air mass resulted in heavy rainfall, which produced localized flash flooding due to the water-logged ground. Most rivers experienced minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the St. Francis River. Low-lying and bottomland woods and fields were inundated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very dry summer, exceptional drought (D4) conditions were experienced area-wide, resulting in heavy losses for local farmers. Things changed in October when widespread heavy rain effectively ended that drought. Widespread 6 to 9 inches of rain fell, with some locations receiving over a foot of rain over the 4 day stretch from October 6 through October 9. By October 9th, the drought was effectively ended by the UNL drought monitor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to widespread dry conditions through the summer and early fall of 2018 most counties experienced extreme to exceptional drought (D3-D4). While some counties saw marked improvement through the late summer and early fall the drought continued into the second week of October. The drought improved area-wide after 6-12 inches of rain fell in a four day stretch in early October. This effectively ended the drought area-wide. While the exact damage costs are unknown, it is estimated that farmer across the entire region suffered millions of dollars of losses due to the extremely dry conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation picked up during August, especially in some of the hardest hit drought areas, but in a lot of cases the damage had already been done, and while the rains did pick back up the ground soil was so parched that it made hardly a dent in the drought across northern Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Howard County reached or maintained D4 drought status for the entire month. While rain did move into the area through the month, the ground was dry enough from the below normal precipitation and above normal temperatures through the month to warrant D4 status maintenance. The direct impact to Howard County is unclear, but statewide drought impacts are estimated around 2 billion dollars, per The University of Missouri Extension Center. The drought has also hurt pastures, with about three-quarters in poor or very poor conditions, according to the USDA report. Many pastures haven't been able to support grazing cattle, prompting farmers to feed cattle with hay that might normally be saved for winter. It's also hurt the hay crop, which is down about one-third from normal. The 2018 drought is turning out small corn ears. Some farmers are not waiting until harvest, instead trying to get the most out of the crop by baling it or cutting it for silage for cattle.  Farmers can now clean out sediment in ponds to increase water-holding capacity. Ponds in the conservation program are built for erosion control.|||Sources:  https://www.stltoday.com/news/local/govt-and-politics/from-drinking-water-to-farms-drought-s-effect-creeping-across/article_35440d14-a1c4-5f86-ac64-b5b63906fe57.html .||https://www.foxnews.com/us/drought-takes-toll-on-missouri-farmers-crops-cattle .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/weather/cattleman-turns-baling-corn-drought .||https://www.missouriruralist.com/conservation/3-conservation-restrictions-lifted-during-drought .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of August 6 a cluster of severe thunderstorms moved into northwest Missouri, and caused some scattered wind damage. The predominant wind gusts from this complex were around 60 to 70 mph winds, along with some tree and power line damage. There was a lone report of severe hail, with golf ball sized hail being reported near Mercer, Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 60 mph wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved east across Mississippi and Alabama, spreading a wintry mix into extreme southern parts of southeast Missouri. Most of the impacts were felt south of U.S. Highway 60, especially from southern Butler County to New Madrid County. The primary type of precipitation was freezing rain, which glazed surfaces with up to one-tenth inch of ice. Roads became slick and hazardous, including Interstate 55. The Missouri State Highway Patrol reported a couple of accidents with injuries in Stoddard and New Madrid Counties. The injuries were not serious.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms and tornadoes developed within a Quasi-Linear Convective System that moved across the region during the late evening of November 30th and continued into the early morning of the 1st.  The parent storm system intensified over the southern Plains and tracked northeast into northern Missouri.  Widespread severe wind gusts between 60 and 80 mph, hail and isolated tornadoes occurred. Seven tornadoes were confirmed over southwest and south central Missouri.  The tornadoes were rated EF-0 to EF-1 based on NWS Storm Surveys.  Unfortunately, there was one fatality that occurred in Aurora, Missouri when a tornado struck a local motel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage occurred to the Meek's Building Supply Company.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly moving line of showers and a few thunderstorms contained locally strong winds. Isolated minor wind damage occurred in association with this line. The convection occurred in association with a strong 500 mb shortwave that ejected northeast from Texas into the mid Mississippi Valley. At the surface, low pressure was centered along the Kansas/Oklahoma border. East of the low, a warm front moved northward across southeast Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deck was blown away, and a large glass table was blown into a neighbor's yard. Although no lightning was observed with the heavy rain showers, they had produced lightning earlier in Carter County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of May 2, 2018 strong thunderstorms moved out of Kansas and into western Missouri. These storms formed squall line tornadoes, that did damage across portions of the Kansas City Metro Area. The bulk of the damage occurred in Jackson County, Missouri, where several trees were destroyed in the Raytown area near and along Raytown Road. Two tornadoes occurred that night in Raytown causing a narrow path of tree damage, which also caused some structure damage.  Several other areas had some tornado and wind damage, although not quite as extensive. Several areas had trees blown down with some damage done to objects they fell onto.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a line of severe thunderstorms pushed through the area, it produced several tornadoes. One of these tornadoes produced damage to large threes, which fell onto houses, causing roof and structural damage. The most notable of these damages occurred in Raytown where a house was significantly damaged by a large fallen tree. There were no injuries reported from these storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms pushed through western and central Missouri on May 19. These storms produced very large hail, as large as baseballs. Strong winds, along with the hail, caused structural damage, mainly across northern Missouri.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of May 2, 2018 strong thunderstorms moved out of Kansas and into western Missouri. These storms formed squall line tornadoes, that did damage across portions of the Kansas City Metro Area. The bulk of the damage occurred in Jackson County, Missouri, where several trees were destroyed in the Raytown area near and along Raytown Road. Two tornadoes occurred that night in Raytown causing a narrow path of tree damage, which also caused some structure damage.  Several other areas had some tornado and wind damage, although not quite as extensive. Several areas had trees blown down with some damage done to objects they fell onto.
A storm produced 3/4 hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee reported 3/4 hail at HWY 152 and North Oak Trafficway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered storms developed along a stationary front. Some of the storms became severe with several reports of wind damage and a funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters tracked what they thought was a tornado. The spotters recorded the event but the trees along the horizon obscured the bottom of the funnel. NWS St. Louis sent a survey team to the area and could not find any evidence of a tornado, so this has been classified as a funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front over the Ozarks interacted with several upper level disturbances to produce showers and thunderstorms with locally heavy rainfall from the morning of the 6th to the afternoon of the 7th.  There was one report of quarter sized hail with storms over Miller County during the afternoon of the 7th.  As the front was lifting northward in response to pressure falls over the southern High Plains, strong to severe storms developed north of the Ozarks. On the morning of the 9th, when the main upper level system shifted eastward a final round of showers and thunderstorms moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 658 flooded along Pigeon Creek and was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On consecutive afternoons and evenings of October 8 and 9, several mini or low-topped supercells formed across NW Missouri. These storms did not produce much in the way of severe weather, aside from some weak and brief tornadoes. The damage was relegated to powerlines and tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado formed from a low-topped supercell and traveled over mainly farmland, downing a few trees across secondary roadways. This tornado crossed into Caldwell County when it crossed Price Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Starting on October 6th, the atmosphere set itself up in a pattern conducive to bring a prolonged period of heavy rain to portions of northeastern Kansas and northwestern Missouri. The heaviest rain occurred Saturday and Sunday (Oct 6-7). By Tuesday night (October 9th) more than 11 inches of rain fell in and around the Kansas City Metro. Local drought conditions were still in the Extreme to Exceptional category going into the event, so flash flooding was relatively low impact, relegated mostly to several road closures. The bigger impact from this prolonged rainfall event came from local river and stream flooding. Several streams entered the moderate to major flooding category. The 4 day event spanning Oct 6-9 resulted in the greatest 4 day rain total in recorded Kansas City History. At Kansas City International Airport 9.79 inches were recorded for the Oct 6-9 time period, beating out Sept 12-15, 1977, which had 9.48 over the 4 day span.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads in and around Trenton were flooded due to heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow began falling early in the morning on November 12. While the snow was generally light - moderate at times - 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the area which caused area roads to become icy, and numerous vehicle accidents ensued. Up to 10 injuries had been reported, and 2 fatalities occurred as a result of the icy roads. News story: https://www.kshb.com/news/local-news/one-killed-in-henry-county-crash-caused-by-slick-roads .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 1 to 3 inches of snow fell across the region area roads became very icy, causing numerous vehicle accidents. One such accident that resulted in a fatality occurred along I-49 (Mile Marker 160.2) in Cass County near the town of Archie around 10:00 am, when a vehicle slid off the road, struck a guardrail, and came back onto the road, where it was subsequently hit by a 18 wheeler. The driver of the vehicle died on the scene and the passenger was transported to a local hospital with minor injuries. ||Missouri Highway Patrol Accident Report: https://www.mshp.dps.missouri.gov/HP68/AccidentDetailsAction?ACC_RPT_NUM=180680656 .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely early season blizzard impacted most of northern and central Missouri on November 25, 2018. The snowstorm was more indicative of a storm that would affect the area in the deep winter, as opposed to the middle to late part of autumn. The storm brought between 6 and 8 inches of snow across far northern Missouri, with 4 to 6 inches across the Kansas City Metro area, and along a good portion of the I-70 corridor through western and central Missouri. Further separating this storm from other typical winter storms was the strong winds that accompanied the heavy snow. Most locations through the region experienced winds in excess of 35 mph at times during the storm. The strongest wind gusts came across northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri, where 50-60 mph winds were recorded in nearby Wyandotte County (Kansas) and Leavenworth County (Kansas) and at Kansas City International Airport.||This snowstorm came exceptionally early in the winter season. Just how historical was this event? Here is some perspective.||Typical Novembers bring around 1.2 of snow each season, however...||Only 7 of the previous 20 Novembers have had accumulating snow in Kansas City. ||The 5.8 of snow broke the record for snowiest November 25 in Kansas City history. The previous record was 3.7, which occurred in 1895.||The 7.6 of snow recorded in Kansas City through November 25 ranks 2nd all time for snowiest Novembers in Kansas City History (Going back to 1888), The snowiest |November in KC history came in 1923, when 9.1 of snow fell during the month.||The 5.8 of snow recorded in Kansas City for November 25 was the 2nd snowiest individual day for the month of November. November 29, 1923 brought 9.1 of snow to Kansas City.||Since 2004, no blizzard warnings had been issued for the Kansas City Metro area in the month of November, until this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions started after a few hours of lightly to moderately falling snow. Once the heavy snow arrived winds gusted up to 41 mph for nearly 4 hours, creating whiteout conditions, officially measured by the ASOS at nearby KMKC and KMCI as sub-quarter mile for that duration. Despite the heavy impacts from this system affecting Thanksgiving weekend return traffic, no serious injuries occurred from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved from Kansas into Central Illinois bringing heavy snows of 4 to 13 inches to the area.  Strong north winds gusted to 40 to 50 mph and combined with the heavy snow to produce widespread blizzard conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple sources of snow amounts of 4 to 6 inches occurred in the county. These snows, combined with strong north winds of over 30 mph with frequent gusts over 40 mph produced blizzard conditions in the county. These conditions started in the afternoon and lasted into the overnight hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the earliest snowfalls on record occurred across southeast Missouri. From two to three inches of snow fell across most of southeast Missouri. Three inches of snow was measured in most of Cape Girardeau County. The snow was mixed with sleet and freezing rain in some areas, mostly south and east of Sikeston. The freezing rain and sleet in those areas held down accumulations to an inch or two. The winter precipitation was caused by a strong 500 mb low that moved northeast from Arkansas to the lower Ohio Valley. At the surface, an inverted trough extended from the southeastern states northwest to the lower Ohio Valley.

The storm brought between 6 and 8 inches of snow across far northern Missouri, with 4 to 6 inches across the Kansas City Metro area, and along a good portion of the I-70 corridor through western and central Missouri. Further separating this storm from other typical winter storms was the strong winds that accompanied the heavy snow. Most locations through the region experienced winds in excess of 35 mph at times during the storm. The strongest wind gusts came across northeast Kansas and northwest Missouri, where 50-60 mph winds were recorded in nearby Wyandotte County (Kansas) and Leavenworth County (Kansas) and at Kansas City International Airport.||This snowstorm came exceptionally early in the winter season. Just how historical was this event? Here is some perspective.||Typical Novembers bring around 1.2 of snow each season, however...||Only 7 of the previous 20 Novembers have had accumulating snow in Kansas City. ||The 5.8 of snow broke the record for snowiest November 25 in Kansas City history. The previous record was 3.7, which occurred in 1895.||The 7.6 of snow recorded in Kansas City through November 25 ranks 2nd all time for snowiest Novembers in Kansas City History (Going back to 1888), The snowiest |November in KC history came in 1923, when 9.1 of snow fell during the month.||The 5.8 of snow recorded in Kansas City for November 25 was the 2nd snowiest individual day for the month of November. November 29, 1923 brought 9.1 of snow to Kansas City.||Since 2004, no blizzard warnings had been issued for the Kansas City Metro area in the month of November, until this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions started after a few hours of lightly to moderately falling snow. Once the heavy snow arrived winds gusted up to 41 mph for nearly 4 hours, creating whiteout conditions, officially measured by the ASOS at nearby KDMO as sub-quarter mile for that duration. Despite the heavy impacts from this system affecting Thanksgiving weekend return traffic, no serious injuries occurred from this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a warm front that was lifting northward through southern Missouri. There were numerous reports of damaging winds with the severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down several trees across southern portions of Ste. Genevieve County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe storms impacted the region during the afternoon and evening of July 19, 2018.  The most significant of which included a derecho that began in north central KS during the morning hours that moved southeast toward Kansas City into the early afternoon and then into southwest Missouri through the early evening hours before ending in northern Arkansas. Most notably were several observations of 70+ mph winds, numerous reports of trees and power lines down, and a mass fatality incident on Table Rock Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted at the intersection of Walnut Lawn and South Campbell Streets. The report was received via social media and the time based on radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were reported downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate Low Top Supercell thunderstorm complex's moved through the Ozarks. The first, during the early morning hours produced hail up to golf balls in size, damaging straight line winds and an EF-0 tornado as it moved through the Missouri Ozarks. The EF-0 tornado downed trees and power lines northwest of Camdenton Missouri. The second complex later produced several reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were uprooted and power-lines downed by winds estimated at 60-70 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two (2) docks were flipped over and a home had significant roof damage. A detached carport was flipped over and destroyed. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two separate Low Top Supercell thunderstorm complex's moved through the Ozarks. The first, during the early morning hours produced hail up to golf balls in size, damaging straight line winds and an EF-0 tornado as it moved through the Missouri Ozarks. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey found numerous large tree limbs were down, trees uprooted and power lines down. This damage was within a larger area of straight line wind damage. Peak wind speeds were estimated at 80 mph. The tornado path length was 0.12 miles and width maximum was 75 yards. The tornado starting location was 7.5 miles NW of Camdenton and ended 7.4 miles NW Camdenton. The tornado was on the ground for duration of approximately 1 minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked along a stalled front across the Mid-South. Precipitation spread across the Mid-South as surface low tracked northeast along the front. Precipitation changed to freezing rain across Northeast Arkansas and the Missouri Bootheel as colder air filtered into the region. Ice accumulations were less than a tenth of an inch with a few slick spots on area roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain created a few slick spots on elevated surfaces such as bridges. Ice accumulations were a tenth of an inch or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front interacted with a very unstable airmass across the Midsouth during the afternoon and evening hours of February 24h. All facets of severe weather occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado tracked from Clay County, AR to Dunklin County. Most of the damage was in Malden where a number of homes were damaged. A few homes, trees and power poles were damaged from the St. Francis River to Malden. The path was intermittent. The tornado crossed into New Madrid County and then lifted. Estimated peak wind along the entire track was 115 mph.
